topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt impossibly heavy in my hand, cold metal digging into my palm. Every creak of the old floorboards above seemed amplified, each one a sharp, unwelcome intrusion. I stood in the doorway, breathing in shallow, quick gasps, my throat suddenly tight. Mom was already in the living room, humming softly, a picture of serene domesticity that felt like a spotlight aimed directly at my unraveling nerves. "I'm here," I managed, the words sounding thin and reedy. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the doorframe for support. I couldn't seem to settle my gaze on anything; my eyes darted from the hallway to the unfamiliar expanse of the room, cataloging escape routes that didn't exist. The silence between us stretched, taut and brittle, threatening to snap.

[story 2]

The sheer volume of familiar clutter in the hallway was overwhelming. Boxes, old photographs spilling from cardboard cartons, the scent of lavender and aged paper—it all swirled together, creating a dizzying vortex in my chest. I kept running my fingers through my hair, a frantic, repetitive motion that did nothing to soothe the internal tremor. When Dad finally looked up from his newspaper, his expression was one of mild, puzzled concern. "Everything alright, honey?" he asked, his voice level, which only seemed to increase the pressure building behind my ribs. I swallowed hard, tasting dust. "Yeah," I lied, the sound barely audible. My feet felt rooted to the spot, unable to move toward the kitchen or the stairs. I just stood there, rigid, waiting for the inevitable shift in the atmosphere, bracing for whatever storm was about to break.

[story 3]

I kept checking the locks, a pointless ritual that only served to heighten the internal alarm bells ringing relentlessly. The air in the house felt thick, suffocating, as if the very molecules were pressing in on me. Every shadow seemed to writhe, taking on shapes that weren't quite right. When Mom offered me a cup of tea, the warmth of the mug felt alien against my suddenly cold skin. I took a large gulp, the liquid burning a momentary path down my throat, and immediately regretted it. My focus narrowed to a single point—a loose thread on the rug—as I tried desperately to anchor myself to something tangible before the entire structure of my composure dissolved. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the wallpaper, but my legs refused to obey.
